narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 28.50, your profile also includes solid upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, but your Year 1 school decile of 4 and especially your Year 3 decile of 2 stood out in the evaluation process. The university's model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, assessed your likelihood of failing at 49.9%—just over the threshold that typically triggers a rejection to minimize risks for incoming law students. This close call meant your application was denied, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass confidently. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile ranking of 2, well below the typical 5.53 average, signaling to the model a significant dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, which is near the dataset average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction in a more favorable direction by showing steady prior achievement. Your LSAT score of 28.50, however, also worked against you to a lesser but notable degree, as it's substantially lower than the average 36.70, reinforcing concerns about exam readiness despite your full-time commitment. Compared to typical students who pass, your later-year grades lag behind those in higher deciles, which is a common pattern the model flags for Asian applicants like yourself who often face high expectations in competitive fields. While your family background is a strength shared by many successful candidates, it couldn't offset the academic red flags here. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many students rebound strongly—but the model weighs these patterns from historical data heavily. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average levels, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or explanations in reapplications, and targeting schools with more holistic reviews. Gathering letters highlighting your resilience as a full-time student from an upper-middle background could also help reframe your story. With targeted improvements, your profile has room to shift that probability in your favor for future applications.
